He tells us what he noticed: not her face first but the margin of her programme, which she was drawing on throughout a particularly extended quadrille. Small beetles, he thought at first. He looked closer. They were ammonites.

He learned about Sir Phillip Morrow four days later, from his uncle, who mentioned it at breakfast as local intelligence. Edmund says he put down his toast. His uncle asked if something was wrong. Edmund said no.

The chapter ends before the first climb. Edmund is outside, looking up at the east face, October already thinning the creeper. He notes, without quite stating, that he had not yet asked himself whether this was reasonable. He had only thought: the window is the one on the right. And then he'd begun.

---

## Chapter Two — *First Entry* (~1000 words)

The first climb in full physical detail — what it costs, what's undignified, what's unexpectedly straightforward. The drainpipe is colder than anticipated. He tears his left cuff on a bracket. At the ledge, he has a moment of pure practical crisis that has nothing to do with Eleanor and everything to do with not falling. He does not fall.

The window is unlatched. This detail stops him for a moment on the ledge. She knew he was coming — he'd sent a note via a boy at the inn, the note itself circumspect to the point of obscurity, and the unlatched window is her answer, which is clearer than any words.

Eleanor is sitting up, still dressed, with a book open on her lap — Lyell's *Principles of Geology*, which strikes Edmund as an unusual choice for midnight reading. She looks at him. He is halfway through the window with a torn cuff and a graze on his left hand. He says something like: *"I thought I should call."* She says: *"You're bleeding on my windowsill."* He looks down. He is.

The conversation that follows is real and particular: Eleanor tells him she knows who he is (she'd asked around too — this pleases him and makes him feel outmanoeuvred simultaneously), that she knows he has no independent fortune, and that she finds Sir Phillip Morrow to be a man whose eyes don't move when he listens to someone. This is her precise, damning description and Edmund recognizes it instantly as exact. She asks him what he wants. He gives an answer that's too long and trails off. She waits. He says the true thing: that he hasn't yet determined how to want what he can't have, and that he thought seeing her might help him determine it. She considers this. She says it's a strange reason to climb a house. He agrees.

He stays two hours. Before he goes, she says: *"The window sticks. You'll need to lift and push simultaneously."* He manages it. On the ground he looks up and she has already stepped back from the glass. Then Harriet's voice from the shadow of the yew hedge, conversational: *"You've got ivy in your hair."*

---

## Chapter Three — *What We Talked About* (~1000 words)

This chapter resists becoming a montage. It is built around one specific evening — Edmund's third visit, mid-October — that stands for the period.

He arrives to find Eleanor in the middle of a frustration: she has been corresponding with a geologist at the Royal Society and his most recent letter has addressed her as *"Dear Sir"* despite four previous exchanges in which her name appeared clearly. She is not performing outrage — she is genuinely, specifically angry, and she shows Edmund the letter. He reads it. He thinks: he could offer consolation or he could take the problem seriously. He takes it seriously. They spend an hour drafting her response — sharp, unanswerable, signed with her full name and a postscript that Edmund talks her into removing. The postscript was better than anything in the letter but it would have made the man her enemy. She knows he's right. She removes it with visible reluctance.

Edmund reflects, in narration, that this is when he understood what was happening to him — not through a romantic access of feeling but through the specific sensation of wanting to read whatever she wrote next. He doesn't say this aloud. He describes a different moment instead: she shows him the ammonites she has arranged on the window ledge, brought up from the riverbed, and explains each one briefly, without performing the explanation, the way you'd explain something to a person you expected to actually follow it. He followed it.

The chapter also gives us Harriet more clearly. She arrives at eleven to report that Mrs. Beale's light is out and has been for twenty minutes, which is the signal that it's safe for Edmund to leave. She stays three minutes and in that time observes Edmund with the air of someone conducting a study. She has decided, she tells Eleanor, that he is *"not uninteresting."* Eleanor says that's very generous. Harriet says she knows.

Edmund leaves. The Virginia creeper is noticeably thinner. He notes this with the beginning of practical concern.

---

## Chapter Four — *Sir Phillip* (~1000 words)

Sir Phillip arrives for a four-day visit in late October. The chapter is Edmund's — he cannot come while the baronet's carriage is in the drive, so we see Phillip only at church on Sunday, across the aisle, and what Edmund sees is not a villain.

Morrow is perhaps fifty, well-preserved, with the easy physical confidence of a man who has never worried about the cost of things. He sits beside Eleanor in the pew. He does not look at her during the service, which could mean propriety or indifference — Edmund cannot determine which from the nave, and this uncertainty bothers him. After the service, in the churchyard, Edmund watches Morrow speak with Vane for ten minutes while Eleanor stands slightly to the side, examining a lichen-covered headstone with the focused attention she gives to geological formations.

Edmund does not approach. He watches Eleanor trace the edge of the lichen with one finger.

He cannot visit for four days. During this time he writes three letters that he doesn't send. He takes long walks. He tries to continue work on the boundary dispute, which his uncle finds much easier to conclude without Edmund's nominal assistance. On the fourth evening, Harriet appears at his inn — alone, which alarms him, she has never done this before — to report that Morrow is leaving in the morning and that Eleanor wants Edmund to come tomorrow night. Harriet delivers this with scientific neutrality. Edmund asks how Eleanor seemed. Harriet considers: *"Like herself, mostly. A little more so."* Edmund doesn't know what this means. He suspects it isn't good.

He comes the next night. The chapter ends as he reaches the ledge — the creeper has lost another section, he's had to improvise a new route, it's taken longer than usual. He's breathing harder than the climb explains. The window is unlatched.

The chapter has not resolved the question of what Eleanor thinks of Morrow. This is deliberate. We'll find out alongside Edmund.

---

## Chapter Five — *What She Said* (~1000 words)

The conversation that follows Edmund's arrival after the Morrow visit. This is the emotional centre of the story and must resist the pull toward a clean declaration scene.

Eleanor is uncharacteristically quiet. Edmund, nervous, talks too much — he asks about Morrow, gets a brief answer, asks a follow-up, gets another brief answer. He notices he's doing this and stops. In the silence, Eleanor says: *"He knew I was interested in geology."* Edmund waits. *"My father told him. He mentioned it once, during dinner."* Another pause. *"He didn't ask me anything about it."* Edmund understands exactly what she means: that Morrow has noted the fact of her interest as one notes a piece of furniture, not asked about it as one asks about a living thing.

Edmund then makes his own partial confession — not an elegant prepared speech but something that comes out in parts and in the wrong order. He has a property in Shropshire, twelve acres, a house that needs its east wing re-roofed. He has a legal practice that is paying its own way as of the previous quarter. He is a second son, which she knows, and he has no expectations beyond what he has made, which is small but real. He says this is not a declaration. He says it's information she should have.

Eleanor is quiet for a moment. Then she asks what the property is called. He tells her. She asks about the land — the geology, specifically. He doesn't know enough to answer properly. She seems to consider this. She asks if she could. Know it properly, she means.

He says yes.

The chapter ends without a formal acceptance because Eleanor doesn't give one. What she gives is a question that assumes a future, which is not quite the same thing, and they both know it. The chapter ends with them sitting in silence that is different from the earlier silences. Edmund does not try to characterize it further.

---

## Chapter Six — *Mrs. Beale* (~1000 words)

November. The chapter opens with Edmund noting that the Virginia creeper is nearly bare — he's had to commit fully to the drainpipe and the ledge, no creeper to assist. It's colder. He's developed a technique for the drainpipe that involves his knees in a way that would be humiliating to describe to anyone who matters.

Harriet's ribbon system has become elaborate. Edmund arrives one evening to find three ribbons, which is not a configuration they've planned for. He stands in the dark trying to remember if three ribbons means anything or nothing. He decides nothing and climbs. He is wrong.

Mrs. Beale is in the upper corridor, moving slowly, testing the handles of each door. Edmund, who has made it through the window and is in the small anteroom adjacent to Eleanor's sitting room, hears her. He does not move for a long time. He counts her footsteps. He stands in the dark with his hand on the interior door handle, listening to a sixty-year-old housekeeper methodically confirm that her house is safe, and he feels — for the first time with clarity — what he is actually doing. He is in this woman's house without invitation. He is conducting himself in a way that, if discovered, would harm Eleanor. He knew this before. He knows it differently now.

Mrs. Beale tries the anteroom door. The handle moves half an inch. Edmund holds it from inside, gentle, no resistance that would register as more than a stiff latch. She moves on.

He waits fifteen minutes before going through to Eleanor. He tells her what happened, including the moment with the door handle. Eleanor doesn't make light of it. She says: *"She's going to find out, Edmund."* He says: *"Not tonight."* She says: *"That's not the same as no."*

This is their first real disagreement. It doesn't resolve. Edmund leaves earlier than usual. The three ribbons meant Mrs. Beale was still moving around; Harriet had made an error in the system. Harriet is not told this was her error, at Edmund's request. He is not sure why he makes this request. He writes this in narration with his characteristic doubling: *"I told myself it was kindness. Perhaps it was."*

---

## Chapter Seven — *Vane* (~1000 words)

The chapter begins wrongly. Edmund receives a note from Harriet — not Eleanor, which is the first signal — in the morning, not the evening, which is the second. The note is brief: *"Tonight. She needs to speak to you. It's important."* No ribbons. No system. Just come.

Edmund comes. The climb is described in two sentences where it's usually given a paragraph — he's rushing, he's worried, something has happened. He doesn't notice until he's on the ledge that there is a light on in the downstairs library. He has noticed this before and it has always meant Vane has retired with a book; it means nothing. He goes in.

Eleanor tells him. Sir Phillip has made his formal offer through the proper channels. Vane has indicated he will accept it on Eleanor's behalf before Christmas. The wedding would be in spring. This is not new information in structure but it is newly immediate, and Eleanor is not distressed in the way Edmund expected — she is precise and purposeful. She has decided something. She tells Edmund she has decided she needs her father to hear from her, directly, that she will not marry Morrow. She does not ask Edmund to be present for this. She is telling him, she says, because he should know.

Edmund says he'll speak to Vane.

Eleanor says: *"Edmund."* A warning.

Edmund says he knows. He'll speak to Vane anyway.

The argument that follows — their real argument — is about whether Eleanor's autonomy requires him to stay silent, or whether his speaking is an act of care rather than appropriation. Neither of them fully wins this argument. Edmund's position is partly right and partly wanting to do something when there is nothing he can do. Eleanor knows this. She says so. He says she's right. He says he'll speak to Vane anyway. She puts her hands over her face briefly.

Then the door opens.

Gerald Vane stands in the doorway — not a servant, him — in his robe, a book in his hand, and the expression of a man who has arrived at an explanation for several things that puzzled him. He looks at Edmund. He looks at his daughter. He says, very quietly: *"Sit down, please."*

What follows is not a gothic confrontation. Vane doesn't shout. Edmund doesn't declaim. Edmund speaks — plainly, in the legal register that arrives when he's frightened — about his circumstances, his intentions, his awareness that he has acted improperly. He gets through most of it. Then he stalls, loses the thread, and in the silence Eleanor tells her father she will not marry Phillip Morrow. She says this clearly, without drama, and she says one further thing: that she has more to say to him on this matter and has tried to say it and not been permitted to finish. She intends to finish.

Vane says nothing for a long moment. He tells Edmund to leave his house. He does not say the county. He does not say do not come back. Edmund understands the difference.

He does not look at Eleanor when he goes. He looks at the doorway. Then he looks at Vane, who is looking at his daughter, and in that look Edmund sees that the man is listening. He goes.

The grounds at night. He does not look up at the window.

---

## Chapter Eight — *The Front Door* (~1000 words)

A fortnight passes in a paragraph — Edmund staying at his uncle's house, working, not returning to the inn where he'd been visible and waiting-adjacent. He receives one note from Harriet: *"He's thinking."* He doesn't know what this costs Harriet to send or whether it's comfort or information or both.

He is called to Ashworth on a Tuesday afternoon, formally, by letter from Gerald Vane. Not invited — called. Edmund notes this word choice in narration without irony.

The front door. Edmund describes it: wide, painted black, a brass knocker in the shape of a fish that he had never registered from his usual approach. A maid answers. He is shown to Vane's study.

The conversation between Edmund and Vane is not warm. Vane is a man who has been moved off a position he held for good reasons — he believed he was securing his daughter's life, and Edmund's accounting of himself is not dazzling — and he has not arrived at fondness for Edmund or comfort with what's happened. What he has arrived at is a question: does Edmund understand what Eleanor's refusing Morrow will mean, socially, in this county, in the short term? Edmund says yes. Vane says he doubts it. Vane tells him. Edmund listens. This costs Edmund something to hear.

Vane's condition: Edmund will not take Eleanor from Derbyshire until his boundary dispute is settled and his uncle comfortable. He is to conduct himself in a manner that allows the neighbourhood to gradually forget this winter. These are not soft conditions — they ask for time and patience and a kind of invisibility. Edmund agrees. Vane says that he knew Edmund's uncle thirty years ago, that he was a man of his word, that he hopes this is a characteristic that passes in families. The meeting is over.

Edmund is shown to the drawing room.

Eleanor is standing at the window — not her window, the tall drawing room window on the ground floor, looking out onto the south lawn. She turns when he comes in. He looks at her face. Her shoulders move first. Then she laughs.

They have twenty minutes before a maid will bring tea and propriety will fully reassert itself. Edmund narrates this in a tone that is quieter than the rest of the story — less wry, more direct. He says: they talked about the Shropshire property. She asked again about the geology. He said she could come and look for herself. She said: she knew.

He does not describe the twenty minutes further. He says: some things are easier not to translate.

The chapter — and the story — ends with a specific image: Edmund leaving Ashworth by the front drive, gravel under his boots, the east face of the manor visible from the path. He glances up at it once — the Virginia creeper bare now, the drainpipe, the second-floor ledge, the window. He can see, from here, how precarious the route actually was. He couldn't see this from the wall.

He turns back to the path.
step_outputs.5:
# Edmund Ashby

## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws

Edmund is the second son of a Shropshire solicitor who died when Edmund was seventeen, leaving the family respectable but thin. The eldest son, Robert, inherited the house and the practice. Edmund was given a good education, a small property that had been his mother's, and the implicit understanding that he would make his own way. He has done this — he read law, established a modest practice in Shrewsbury, and is, at twenty-four, genuinely building something — but the process has made him careful with money in ways that occasionally shade into anxiety, and has given him a particular sensitivity to the unspoken social arithmetic of rooms: who has more than whom, who knows it, who is pretending not to.

He is a good lawyer because he listens well and because he has a tolerance for the boring middle sections of things — the parts where nothing is happening but which determine everything that follows. He applies this to people. He will wait out a silence that would make other men speak. This is sometimes a gift and occasionally maddening.

His idiosyncrasies: he owns too many notebooks and uses none of them consistently, so important information is scattered across seven or eight half-filled volumes he can never locate when needed. He has a habit, when thinking, of tapping the second finger of his left hand against his thumbnail — not nervously, just rhythmically, as if counting something out. He is a better cook than he should be for a man of his class and era, a skill acquired from necessity during his first year of independent living, which he finds privately useful and publicly conceals. He cannot whistle. He has tried. He has genuinely practiced. He cannot do it.

His flaw — the real one, not the charming version — is that he sometimes acts on a decision before he has admitted to himself that he has made it. He tells himself he is still deliberating while his body is already doing the thing. This is not quite dishonesty but it is a way of avoiding self-knowledge that has consequences. The window-climbing is the most dramatic instance. The conversation with Vane in chapter seven is another. He does things and then discovers, sometimes too late, what he meant by them. Eleanor, who processes everything consciously and explicitly, finds this trait both frustrating and periodically alarming.

He was not, before Eleanor, a man who did reckless things. This is the part he finds hardest to explain, looking back.

## Physicality

Tallish — six feet, approximately, which he's slightly self-conscious about because he takes up more space than he means to. He has a lean, angular build that suggests he didn't always eat quite enough in his early twenties, and large hands with long fingers that he doesn't know what to do with in formal social situations. His face is pleasant rather than striking — brown eyes, a slightly crooked nose from a boyhood encounter with a low doorframe, brown hair that he keeps meaning to have cut. He moves carefully in new spaces, ducking slightly at doorways out of habit even when there's no need, which gives him an air of perpetual mild apology that is not inaccurate. When he sits, he tends to lean slightly forward, elbows on knees — a posture his mother called the lawsuit position.

He is good with his hands in a practical sense — the climbing, but also things like untying knots, fixing small mechanisms. He is not graceful but he is precise.

## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships

Edmund's primary allegiance, at the story's start, is to the version of his life he has been carefully constructing: the practice, the property, the slow accumulation of something stable. Eleanor disrupts this not by making him want to abandon it but by making him see that it was a structure built around an absence he hadn't named. He wants her specifically — not rescue, not romance in the abstract — and this specificity is what he cannot reason his way out of.

He is close to his uncle Clement, who is kind and vague and thinks better of Edmund than Edmund's record quite supports. His relationship with his brother Robert is cordial and slightly stiff, the stiffness of two people who care about each other and have learned, over years, which subjects to avoid. His mother writes him long letters full of local news and embedded practical advice that she doesn't identify as advice.

His allegiance to Eleanor is total once it's formed, but it arrives before he's examined it — which is his pattern. He is loyal to her before he has consciously decided to be.

## Voice

*Flavour dialogue:*

"The first time, I told myself I was merely returning a book she'd mentioned. I had no book. I had not obtained a book. I am not sure at what point I stopped considering this a relevant detail."

"She asked me once why I became a solicitor. I said it was because I was good at reading documents no one else wanted to read. She said that wasn't why. I said: no, it wasn't. She waited. I said I liked knowing where things stood, legally. Who owned what. What was owed and to whom. She said she thought that was very sad. I said I thought so too, rather. We didn't discuss it further."

*Thinking, outside the manor wall:*
"The window on the right. Third floor — no, second, count the floors, Ashby. Second. The one that sticks. She left it unlatched. That is a fact. That is an act. Stop looking at the ground."

---

# Eleanor Vane

## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws

Eleanor is the elder daughter of Gerald Vane and his late wife Catherine, who died of a fever when Eleanor was fourteen. She was, before her mother's death, being educated in the standard way — languages, pianoforte, watercolour, the management of a household. Afterward, her education became eccentric because her father, grieving and distracted, didn't monitor it closely, and Eleanor filled the gap with whatever was in the library. The library at Ashworth is good — Gerald Vane's father was a collector — and Eleanor read through it without a system, which means she has wide and oddly patchy knowledge: strong on natural history, geology, and eighteenth-century travel writing; weak on the novel; entirely without the accomplishments that would make her legible in a drawing room.

She plays the pianoforte adequately and doesn't enjoy it. She does not embroider. She speaks serviceable French and better German, the German acquired from a governess who stayed three years and whom Eleanor liked. She draws constantly in the margins of things — not as art but as notation, as a way of thinking. Her sketchbooks, which she doesn't call sketchbooks, are full of annotated geological formations, cross-sections of rock strata, beetles labelled with their Latin names, and occasional, unannounced small portraits of people she's been talking to. She has never shown Edmund the portrait she made of him. It is a decent likeness.

Her personality is direct in a way that reads, to people who don't know her, as coldness. She doesn't perform warmth she doesn't feel, which is unusual and occasionally startling. The warmth she does feel, she feels entirely — she is, to the three people she trusts, transparently and quietly devoted. She doesn't say this. She shows it by paying attention, specifically and persistently, to what they actually say rather than what she expects them to say.

Her idiosyncrasies: she has strong opinions about windows, specifically the proportion of window to wall in a room, and will notice and comment on this in any new space. She cannot stand the smell of carnations, which she associates with her mother's funeral. She organizes the ammonites on her windowsill by age — oldest nearest the wall, youngest nearest the glass — and has corrected visitors who moved them, on three occasions, without explaining why they needed correcting. She has a very good memory for text — near-perfect recall for passages she's read closely — and uses this occasionally in conversation in a way that can feel like evidence being introduced.

Her flaw is this: she sometimes trusts her own analysis of a situation over the evidence in front of her, particularly when the evidence is emotional rather than factual. She decided, early in the story, that she would not marry Morrow — this is correct. She decided, also early, that her father's objection to Edmund was entirely about money and class — this is partly right and she is partially wrong about what Vane is actually afraid of, and this gap in her understanding makes chapter seven harder than it needed to be. She is not infallible and she doesn't think she is, but she has a particular blind spot around the emotional logic of people she's decided to be disappointed in.

She finishes people's sentences when they're taking too long — not to be rude, but because her mind arrives at the end before they do and patience is a skill she has to consciously apply. She applies it inconsistently.

## Physicality

Eleanor is medium height, dark-haired, with pale skin that colours distinctly when she's annoyed — high in the cheeks, extending slightly to the throat. Her hands are always slightly ink-stained, and she has a small callus on the middle finger of her right hand from writing. She is not considered a conventional beauty in the county: her face is too specific, all strong brows and an expressive, mobile mouth that doesn't hold still during other people's conversations. She tends to stand close to walls at social events, not from shyness but from a preference for the perimeter, from which the whole room is visible.

Her laugh — Edmund notes this — starts in her shoulders before it becomes sound, a brief convulsion, nearly silent, before the actual laugh arrives. This takes people by surprise the first time. He is observing this from the window ledge the first time he sees it and nearly loses his footing.

She wears her hair simply and has done since fifteen. She has strong opinions about this.

## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships

Eleanor's primary motive is self-determination, which is not the same as freedom — she understands perfectly the constraints of her world and isn't naive about them. What she wants is to make her own choices within those constraints, and she is unsentimentally clear-eyed about what each choice costs. She leaves the window unlatched because she has decided to — not impulsively, but after considering it for four days after receiving Edmund's note. She is not swept away. She is deliberate, which makes everything she does more significant than impulsiveness would.

She is closest to Harriet, to whom she is both sister and something approaching a parent — she has been, since their mother died, the person who monitors Harriet's education, worries about Harriet's recklessness, and occasionally has to be prevailed upon by Harriet to take reckless actions herself. She loved her mother fiercely and does not speak about her. She has a complicated, largely non-verbal relationship with her father — she knows he loves her, she knows his love expresses itself through provision and arrangement rather than conversation, and she has been angry about this for eight years without ever saying so directly.

## Voice

*Flavour dialogue:*

"The letter said 'Dear Sir.' I have written to him four times. My name is on each letter. It begins with a vowel, which I grant you could lead to some ambiguity, but — " she put the letter down. "He knows."

"My father thinks I don't understand what I'm giving up. He's wrong. I understand it precisely. That's rather the point."

*To Edmund, after he's talked around something for too long:*
"Edmund. Say the actual thing."

*Internal, regarding the window being left unlatched:*
She had decided on the Tuesday. She'd changed her mind on the Wednesday, changed it back on the Thursday, and woken on Friday with the certainty of someone who has finished an argument with themselves. She did not examine this too closely. Some decisions clarify best when you stop looking directly at them.

---

# Gerald Vane

## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws

Gerald Vane inherited Ashworth at thirty-two, married well at thirty-four, and was, for approximately twelve years, a man who enjoyed his life. His wife Catherine was sharper than him and warmer, and their marriage was, by the standards of their class and time, genuinely affectionate. Her death remade him in ways he doesn't have language for — he became more formal, more procedural, more focused on arrangement and outcome. He has spent the fifteen years since ensuring that everything which can be secured is secured. He loves his daughters in the way this rebuilt version of himself knows how to love: by worrying about their futures and trying to fix them.

He is not stupid. He was, before Catherine died, apparently quite good at conversation. He retains vestiges of this — he can still be excellent company for twenty-minute intervals at dinner, particularly on the subjects of land management and the history of the county — but he loses it under pressure, and the thing that now makes him uncomfortable is any conversation with emotional content that he hasn't prepared for.

His idiosyncrasies: he keeps every household account personally, in a particular ledger, in a hand so small it requires a glass to read. He wakes at five regardless of season and reads for an hour before the house stirs — this is relevant because it means he is sometimes in the library at midnight with insomnia, which is how he discovers Edmund. He has a slight tic of touching his watch chain when uncertain — not looking at the watch, just making contact with the chain, reassuring himself it's there. He eats the same breakfast every morning without apparent boredom.

His flaw is a failure of imagination about the interior lives of people he is responsible for. He does not ask Eleanor questions because he believes that if something were seriously wrong, he would be able to see it — and he cannot see it, because Eleanor learned young not to show it. He is operating, throughout the story, on a model of his daughter that is eight years out of date, and this is not cruelty but it is a genuine failure, and Eleanor knows it, and it is one of the few things she has not forgiven him for.

He is not wrong about everything. He is right that refusing Morrow will cost Eleanor something socially. He is right that Edmund is a risk. He is wrong about whether Eleanor is capable of calculating these risks herself.

## Physicality

Vane is fifty-eight, heavy in the shoulders, with grey hair that he keeps very neat and a face that has the composed, slightly set quality of a man who decided, at some point, that his expressions were not information he wished to share freely. He has Eleanor's strong brows, which on him look like authority and on her look like argument. He moves through his own house with the ease of long ownership — he knows every room, every corridor, and his footsteps in the upper hall are distinctive and known to Edmund by chapter six: even, measured, unhurried.

When he is genuinely surprised, his face changes completely before he reasserts control — a brief, unguarded look that Edmund sees in chapter seven when the sitting room door opens.

## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships

Vane's motive is order — specifically, the continuation of the ordered life he has reconstructed after loss. The Morrow match is, to him, security in the most literal sense: a good man, a good house, a good income. He has not asked Eleanor whether she finds Morrow interesting because the question wouldn't occur to him as relevant. He found Catherine interesting, but that was luck, and he doesn't think it's reasonable to ask luck to repeat itself.

His relationship with Edmund, after chapter seven, is complicated by the fact that Edmund reminds him of someone — not Catherine, but himself at twenty-four, before he had anything, building toward something from almost nothing. He doesn't mention this to anyone.

## Voice

"I am not opposed to your — situation, Mr. Ashby. I am opposed to the manner of it. There is a difference, and I would ask you to observe it."

*To Eleanor, offstage, during the fortnight between chapters seven and eight — a scene we don't see but can infer:*
"I am not asking you to be grateful for my efforts on your behalf. I am asking you to understand that they were efforts."

*Internal, after Edmund leaves in chapter seven:*
He stood in the doorway for a moment after the sound of the front door. Eleanor was still in the room. She was looking at the window — the window, of all things. He thought: I know what I would have done at his age. He did not find this thought comfortable.

---

# Harriet Vane

## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws

Harriet is sixteen and has been, since her mother died, the beneficiary of Eleanor's devoted attention and the subject of Eleanor's most sustained worry. She has also been, since approximately age twelve, conducting a private study of the people around her, which she records in small notebooks she keeps in her left boot when she doesn't want Eleanor to find them. She is not malicious. She is genuinely, constitutionally curious in a way that has not yet found a sanctioned outlet, and so it has taken the form of unauthorized observation.

She is cleverer than she is given credit for, which she is aware of and doesn't mind — being underestimated is mostly useful. She reads widely, borrows Eleanor's geology texts and returns them with questions that Eleanor finds surprising, and has a particular, somewhat alarming interest in the law, which she pursues by asking Edmund questions whenever she has access to him.

Her involvement in Edmund and Eleanor's correspondence begins as observation and becomes investment — not because she finds it romantic (her notes on the affair are clinical) but because she has decided Edmund is the most interesting person to enter the household since the German governess, and she wants to see what happens. This is close enough to rooting for someone that the distinction barely matters.

Her idiosyncrasies: she does not knock before entering rooms, a habit Eleanor has been trying to correct for years. She has strong views on punctuation, specifically the semicolon, which she believes is overused. She has memorized the coach timetables for the county and can recite departure times as other girls recite poetry.

Her flaw — relevant to the story — is that she assumes her models of situations are more complete than they are. She designed the ribbon system without accounting for the possibility that she herself might be the source of error. When her mistake in chapter six is identified, she does not take it well because she is sixteen and has not yet learned to absorb failure with grace. She becomes, briefly, stiff and over-formal, which is how she shows distress.

She will, in Edmund's retrospective estimation, be formidable at thirty. He means this entirely as a compliment.

## Physicality

Harriet is slight and dark-haired like Eleanor, but taller for her age, with a bony, angular frame that she hasn't grown into yet. She moves fast and tends to appear in doorways without announcement. She has a habit of tilting her head slightly to the right when listening, as if triangulating something. Her expression in repose looks like mild skepticism, which is not always a pose.

## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships

Primary allegiance to Eleanor — total, long-standing, and not conditional on anything. Secondary interest in Edmund as a person — she has decided to form a view of him and is in the process of forming it. She is not operating primarily on Eleanor's behalf when she helps: she is operating on her own curiosity, which happens to be aligned with Eleanor's interests. This is not quite selfless and she knows it.

She has a quieter, more difficult relationship with her father than Eleanor does — she is younger and so he is more comfortable with her, paradoxically, because she requires less. She is not yet old enough to arrange.

## Voice

"You climbed the drainpipe. I watched. The bit near the bracket — you need to transfer your weight earlier. You're doing it too late and it's going to be a problem when it's wet."

"I'm keeping notes. Not about anything private. About how people behave in unusual circumstances. You're a useful case."

"Eleanor says you're kind. She doesn't say things like that unless they're exactly true. She's not — she doesn't approximate."

---

# Mrs. Beale

## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws

Mrs. Beale has kept Ashworth running for twenty-two years. She arrived at thirty-eight as housekeeper, outlasted Catherine Vane's illness, managed the household through the years of Vane's withdrawal, raised (practically speaking) a rotating series of maids and footmen, and has opinions about the household and its inhabitants that she expresses to no one, because she understands that a housekeeper's most valuable professional quality is knowing what to keep.

She liked Catherine Vane extremely and would not say so to Gerald, because it would be an intrusion. She is fond of Eleanor in the manner of someone who has watched a child grow and will not call it fondness because that too would be an intrusion. She is the most professionally dignified person in the story.

She is not a woman to be made a fool of. She knows something is wrong in the house before she knows what — a window not quite as she left it, the faint smell of outdoor air in a closed room, a mud smear on the ledge she cannot account for. She does not jump to conclusions because she has learned, over decades, that jumped conclusions create domestic crises that have to be managed afterward. She gathers information slowly and then she is very sure.

Her idiosyncrasy: she locks the same three doors every night in the same order, and if she has to break the sequence, she starts again from the first. Edmund, who does not know this, sits in the anteroom in chapter six while she re-locks two doors she has already locked and he nearly gives himself away shifting his weight.

Her flaw, if it can be called that, is that her loyalty to the family sometimes means she waits longer than she should before acting. She knew, by chapter six, that someone was coming in through a window. She did not tell Vane because she was not yet certain and because the conclusion, once she told it, could not be untold. She tells herself she is waiting for certainty. She is partly waiting because she is not sure what she wants the certainty to mean.

## Physicality

Mrs. Beale is sixty, compact, grey-haired, and carries herself with the absolute assurance of someone who knows exactly where she is in every room she's ever been in. She moves quietly — not stealthily, just without waste. Her face is almost entirely unrevealing, which is a professional skill she has long since lost the ability to turn off.

She wears black as a matter of course. She smells faintly of linen and beeswax and something Edmund, who encountered her in darkness, would later be unable to name.

## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships

Mrs. Beale's loyalty is to the Vane household — not to Gerald Vane specifically, and not to the idea of the household, but to the actual people in it as they actually are. This distinction is important. She is, in chapter six, trying to determine what it is she is actually loyal to: the proprieties of the house, or Eleanor's specific situation, or something that might encompass both. She has not resolved this when Vane resolves it for her by opening the door himself.

After the discovery, she does not discuss what she saw with anyone. Not because she was told not to. Because that's who she is.

## Voice

*Reported in Edmund's narration:*
"She tried the door once. Not twice. If she'd tried it twice I would have known she suspected. Once is habit. I have replayed this distinction in my mind more than I should have."

*What Edmund imagines she might say, if she were the kind of person who said such things:*
"I have not kept this house for twenty-two years by pretending not to notice things. I have kept it by deciding, carefully, what to do with what I notice."
step_outputs.6:
# Chapter One — *The Art*

One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows.

The ivy is the first consideration — Virginia creeper, in the case of Ashworth, which presents beautifully in September, a full crimson flush across the east face that would satisfy any painter, and which becomes, by the third week of October, a treacherous and dwindling resource. The leaves go first, then the thinner stems, and what remains is a skeleton that will bear weight only where it has had decades to establish itself against the stone. One learns to test before committing. One learns which sections are decorative and which are structural. One learns this the way one learns most things of genuine practical value: through an error that stops just short of being serious.

The drainpipe is more reliable, being cast iron and fixed, though it complains. A particular groan at the upper bracket, audible on cold nights, must be managed by pausing on the lower section and allowing the pipe to settle before continuing. This sounds more difficult than it is. Most things do.

From the pipe, one reaches the second-floor ledge — dressed stone, eight inches deep, the width of a careful boot — and from there the window, which is the sitting room window, third from the left on the east face, and which sticks. It must be lifted slightly and then pushed, in a single motion, not sequentially. This took three attempts to determine. I conducted these attempts on the same evening, which was a choice I do not, in retrospect, endorse.

I was twenty-four years old. I had come to Ashworth-on-Derwent in Derbyshire to assist my uncle Clement in settling a boundary dispute that required, by any reasonable legal estimate, a fortnight. I was still there at the end of November. My uncle had by that point largely stopped requiring my assistance, and I had largely stopped pretending to provide it, and we had arrived at a companionable fiction that suited us both, or at any rate suited me, and my uncle Clement is a man of considerable kindness who has never in my memory withdrawn a courtesy once extended.

I met Eleanor Vane at the Brentwood Assembly on the fourteenth of August. I know the date precisely not because I understood its significance at the time but because I had written a letter to my mother that same evening — a letter about my uncle's house, the progress of the boundary dispute, the notable flatness of the Derbyshire social season — and I had dated it, as one dates letters, and the date was therefore preserved in my mother's correspondence box, where I found it eleven years later and confirmed what I had only remembered approximately.

What I noticed first at the Brentwood Assembly was not Eleanor's face but her programme. She was standing at the edge of the room during an extended quadrille — the kind of quadrille that continues long past the point at which everyone involved has ceased to enjoy it — and she was drawing on the margin of her programme with the focused inattention of someone who has found something more interesting than their surroundings. I was near enough to see the drawings but not, for some moments, to identify them. Small, regular, detailed. Shells, I thought initially, or perhaps decorative scrollwork of the kind young ladies are taught to produce.

I moved slightly. I looked more carefully. They were ammonites. Each one annotated in a hand too small to read at distance.

I did not introduce myself that evening. She was with a party and I was not, and my uncle had already retired, and I told myself there was no particular reason to cross a room. I find, examining this memory now with the mild suspicion it deserves, that I was actually already slightly frightened, which is not a condition I have ever found easy to admit.

I learned about Sir Phillip Morrow four days later. My uncle mentioned it at breakfast, as local intelligence, in the way that people who have lived long enough in a place report its news — without drama, expecting interest rather than reaction. Sir Phillip Morrow, baronet, of Morrow Park in Cheshire, had been in correspondence with Gerald Vane of Ashworth. There was understood to be a proposal forthcoming before Christmas. The eldest daughter was a nice girl, very like her mother, though rather bookish — this last offered not as criticism but as fact, with the slight bewilderment of a man describing a feature of the landscape.

I put down my toast. My uncle asked if something was wrong with it. I said no. I picked the toast back up.

I would like to report that what followed was a period of serious reflection in which I weighed the relevant considerations — my finances, my prospects, the impropriety of the situation, the improbability of a successful outcome — and arrived, through careful deliberation, at a reasoned decision. This is not accurate. What actually happened is that I took long walks and thought about the ammonites on the programme, and one evening approximately ten days after the assembly I found myself at the base of the east face of Ashworth Manor at half past ten at night, looking up at the Virginia creeper with a professional eye.

The window on the right, I thought. Second floor. The one that sticks.

I had sent a note, earlier that day, via a boy at the Three Bells inn. The note was brief and sufficiently obscure that it could have meant almost anything. What it meant was: I would like to come tonight, if you will have me. I had not expected a written reply, and I received none. What I received, when I reached the ledge at twenty minutes to eleven and looked at the window, was the sight of the latch sitting open.

She had left it unlatched. I stood on the ledge in the October dark, a slight wind moving through what remained of the creeper, and I understood that the note had been clear enough after all, and that her answer was clearer still.

I lifted and pushed, simultaneously. The window opened.
step_outputs.7:
# Chapter Two — *First Entry*

The drainpipe, up close, is colder than anticipated. This seems obvious in retrospect — cast iron, October, the better part of midnight — but there is a category of obvious thing that only becomes real through contact, and the cold of that pipe against my palms was the first moment the evening stopped being a plan and became an event. I had good gloves. I had left them at my uncle's house. I had not, in the planning stages of the evening, been thinking very clearly about gloves.

The climb itself is not, I should say, dramatic. I am tallish and my arms are longer than they look, and the drainpipe is fixed at four-foot intervals to the stone, which provides both the groaning I have already described and a series of reliable footholds. What it is not is dignified. By the time I reached the bracket at the upper section I had both knees involved in a way that served function entirely at the expense of appearance, and I had acquired, on my left cuff, a tear of approximately two inches from a bolt head that protruded further than it had any professional reason to. I noted the tear. I continued.

The ledge was better. Eight inches of dressed stone and a question of balance rather than grip, which suited me more. I have always had reasonable balance. I moved along it in the way I had planned — facing the wall, both hands flat against the stone — and arrived at the window, which was the third from the left and which was, as I had seen from below, unlatched.

I stopped.

I want to be precise about this pause, because I have thought about it since and I am still not entirely sure what it contained. I was on a ledge, at height, in the dark, outside a house I had no formal invitation to enter. The window before me was unlatched because a woman I had spoken to once, briefly, at a public assembly had received an obscure note from a man she barely knew and had chosen to leave a window open. I understood this to mean she wanted me to come in. I understood also that I could be wrong — that an unlatched window was not a signed document, that I was supplying an interpretation rather than reading a fact.

Then I thought: she annotates her ammonites. In the margin of a programme, in a crowded room, she annotates her ammonites. She does not do things without intention.

I lifted and pushed, simultaneously. The window opened.

She was sitting up. Still dressed — not as she would have been downstairs, the formal arrangement for company, but in a dark wool gown with her hair simply done, as though she had prepared for an ordinary evening and simply not ended it. A book was open on her lap. I had just enough time to register the title — Lyell's *Principles of Geology*, the second volume — before she looked up at me, which was the first time I saw her face at close range and without a crowd around it.

I was halfway through the window. My left cuff was torn. My hands, which I could now see in the candlelight, were reddened from the pipe and there was a graze across the left palm where I'd caught the bracket wrong at the upper section.

I said: "I thought I should call."

She looked at me for a moment. Then she looked at my hand. She said: "You're bleeding on my windowsill."

I looked down. I was.

She set the Lyell aside and produced, from somewhere with the efficiency of a person who had considered that this might happen, a folded cloth. She held it out. I completed my entry — there is no graceful way to finish climbing through a window; one simply commits — and took the cloth and applied it to my palm, and we regarded each other in the way that two people regard each other when the situation they have arranged has now arrived and must be inhabited.

She had asked around, she told me. She said this plainly, without embarrassment, as though research were the obvious response to an obscure note from a slight acquaintance. She knew who my uncle was. She knew the nature of the boundary dispute. She knew I was a solicitor in Shrewsbury, that I was a second son, that I had no independent fortune beyond a small property in Shropshire. She said this not as an accusation but as a ledger — here is what I have established, now proceed. I felt simultaneously impressed and thoroughly outmanoeuvred, which I have since identified as the characteristic sensation of Eleanor's company.

I said I didn't know what to make of Sir Phillip Morrow. It was not what I had intended to say first, but it came out.

She considered this as a legitimate opening. She said that she had dined with him twice and attended the same church service on four occasions, and that what she had observed was a man whose eyes, when someone was speaking to him, did not move. They stayed fixed on a point roughly at the speaker's collarbone. *He's taking in sound*, she said, *but he isn't listening*. She said the distinction was important to her.

I said I thought I understood.

She said: "What is it you want, Mr. Ashby?"

The question was direct and deserved a direct answer and I failed to provide one. I gave instead an answer that began in three different places and arrived nowhere in particular, and I was aware of this as it was happening and could not stop it, and she waited through the entirety of it with the focused patience of someone who has decided to let a process complete before intervening.

When I stopped, I said the true thing. I said I hadn't yet determined how to want what I couldn't have, and I had thought that seeing her might help me determine it.

She was quiet for a moment. Then she said it was a very strange reason to climb a house.

I said I agreed with her.

We talked for two hours. About Lyell, about my property in Shropshire, about the boundary dispute and its finer absurdities, about a geological survey she had been conducting along the Derwent's eastern bank, about whether the law was, as a profession, essentially optimistic or essentially pessimistic about human nature. I said essentially pessimistic. She said she thought that was probably right but that I didn't seem it. I said I was better at concealing it in person.

Before I left she said: "The window sticks. You need to lift and push simultaneously." I told her I had determined this independently. She said she knew, she had heard me determine it, three times, from inside the room.

I went out the way I had come. The descent is easier than the ascent — weight working with you rather than against — and I managed the drainpipe in better time. I was on the ground, looking up at the dark east face, when her window went dark. She had waited until I was down.

Then, from the shadow of the yew hedge to my left, a voice, conversational: "You've got ivy in your hair."

I turned. A girl — young, slight, with Eleanor's dark colouring and an expression of calm scientific interest — was standing six feet away, hands folded, watching me with the air of someone concluding a field observation.

"You're better going down than coming up," she said. "The bit at the upper bracket — you were hesitating. You need to commit earlier."

I said: "You must be Harriet."

She said: "You should get that cuff seen to."

Then she walked back toward the house, unhurried, and I stood in the dark with ivy in my hair and a torn sleeve and a grazed palm, and felt, obscurely, that I had been assessed and that the assessment was not yet complete.
step_outputs.8:
# Chapter Three — *What We Talked About*

There is a particular kind of October evening in Derbyshire that arrives without warning — the temperature drops fifteen degrees between afternoon and dark, the wind finds gaps in clothing it had previously ignored, and the stars, if there are stars, are the hard bright stars of genuine cold rather than the soft ones of summer. My third visit fell on such an evening. I noted this at the time primarily as it related to the drainpipe, which had become, in the cold, a more austere proposition than it had been in the relative warmth of my first two attempts.

The Virginia creeper had lost another section. I had been monitoring its decline with the attention of a man with a professional interest in the matter, which I now was. The section above the first-floor lintel, which had been thinning since my first visit, had given up entirely and taken a portion of adjacent stem with it in a way that required me to revise my route leftward by approximately three feet, adding time and a brief moment of commitment at the transition to the drainpipe that I will not describe in detail.

Harriet's ribbon in the right window: white, single, left side of the frame. All clear.

I had been developing, by this third visit, a cautious trust in the ribbon system, which was Harriet's design and which she had explained to me via a note delivered with the thoroughness of someone who had given the matter serious structural thought. White ribbon, left window, left side: Mrs. Beale's light out, upper floor clear. White ribbon, left window, right side: light out but recent — wait twenty minutes. No ribbon: clear by other intelligence. Red ribbon, either window: do not come. I had asked, via return note, what I should do if there were no ribbon and no signal of any kind. Harriet had replied: *use your judgment*, which was both reassuring and not.

I came in through the window to find Eleanor not reading but standing at the small writing desk in the corner of the room, her back to me, holding a letter in a way that communicated, through the particular tension in her shoulders, that the letter had annoyed her considerably.

I said: "Good evening."

She turned and held the letter out without preamble. "Read this."

It was a letter from a Dr. Archibald Humphrey of the Royal Society's geological committee, responding to Eleanor's most recent inquiry regarding the Derwent valley stratigraphy. It was three paragraphs long. It was addressed to *Dear Sir*.

I read it. I read it again.

"This is the fifth letter," Eleanor said. "My name appears in the first line of each of the previous four. My name, which is Eleanor, which does not — I am aware — announce its gender with absolute clarity, but which I followed, in the second letter, with the clarifying phrase *Miss Eleanor Vane*, specifically to prevent this."

I looked at the letter. I looked at her. I said: "He knows."

"Yes," she said. "He knows."

I considered the available responses. I could have offered consolation — this was unjust, she was right to be angry, the Royal Society's committee was behind the times in its habits. All of this was true and none of it was useful. Instead I said: "Do you want to answer it?"

She looked at me. "I have answered four."

"Do you want to answer it correctly?"

A pause. She set the letter on the desk. "Sit down," she said.

We spent the next hour on the letter. Not drafting, precisely — Eleanor knew what she wanted to say and said it, rapidly, in a hand that moved faster than I could follow — but negotiating, which is the better word. She would write a line and read it to me, and I would tell her whether it was legally sustainable, by which I meant whether it would make Humphrey her enemy or merely her correspondent, and she would revise it or argue with me about it, and we would revise it again. The draft went through four versions. The final letter was precise, unanswerable, and expressed through the mechanism of impeccable courtesy a degree of contempt that I found genuinely impressive.

There was a postscript.

The postscript said, in its entirety: *I note that your committee's published guidelines for correspondence specify that respondents should address inquirers by their stated name. I have stated mine.*

I read it. I said: "You can't send this."

She said: "It's accurate."

"It is," I said. "You still can't send it. He will decide you are a difficult woman and he will stop answering entirely, and you will have been right and have nothing to show for it."

She was quiet for a moment. I watched her look at the postscript. I watched her consider it.

She crossed it out. Slowly, with a kind of precision that was not the same as agreement. She did not say I was right. We both understood that I was right and that this was not, in the fullest sense, the point.

"There," she said. "Sent."

"It isn't sent yet."

"It is in every way that matters."

She sealed it and set it aside and turned to the window ledge, where the ammonites were arranged in their particular order — oldest nearest the wall, youngest nearest the glass — and she seemed, in the way she moved toward them, to be settling something in herself. She picked one up and turned it over in her hands.

"This one is three hundred and eighty million years old," she said. "Approximately." She said *approximately* in the tone of someone who found the approximation personally frustrating. "It came from the lower bank, below the second bend. The strata there are Devonian — unusual for this valley, most of the accessible material is Carboniferous." She set it down. "You'd need to come down to the river to see the exposure properly."

She said this as you say something to a person you expect to be there — not as an invitation requiring acceptance but as a statement of future fact. I noted this. I filed it.

I told her about the Shropshire property — she had not asked again, but it seemed relevant — and she listened in the way she listened, which was with her whole attention and without the social performance of attention, no nodding or small affirmations, just the quality of someone actually receiving information.

Eleven o'clock came. Harriet appeared at the door — she arrived in doors without announcement, simply materialised in them — and said that Mrs. Beale's light had gone out and had been out for twenty minutes, which meant it was safe, which meant it was time.

She stayed three minutes, during which she observed Edmund — myself — with the focused interest of a naturalist recording behaviour. As she left, she said to Eleanor: "He listens better than most people."

Eleanor said: "I know."

Harriet considered me a moment longer. She said: "Not uninteresting." She said it as a conclusion, as though she had been running the calculation since the night of the yew hedge and had now arrived at a result she was prepared to commit to. Then she was gone.

I went out the window into the cold, the creeper skeletal and the stars hard, and I thought about the postscript she had crossed out — slowly, with that precise, dissatisfied hand — and I thought: I would like to read everything she has ever written. All of it. The letters and the annotations and whatever is in those margins.

It was, I recognise now, already too late by then. It had probably been too late since the ammonites on the programme. But the third visit was when I understood it, which is a different thing, and the feeling was less like falling than like looking down and finding you had been standing at the edge for some time.
step_outputs.9:
# Chapter Four — *Sir Phillip*

He arrived on a Wednesday, which I know because Wednesday was the day my uncle and I had established for reviewing the boundary dispute's documentation — a ritual that had become, by late October, more companionable than professional, the papers spread on the table mostly for form's sake while my uncle talked about the history of the county and I attempted to concentrate. I was attempting to concentrate on the morning of the Wednesday in question when I heard a carriage on the road below the window, and I looked up, and through the bare trees I could see the Ashworth drive, and on the Ashworth drive was a travelling carriage of a quality that did not belong to anyone local I had yet identified.

I looked at it for longer than the boundary documentation warranted. My uncle said something about the disputed fence line. I said yes, quite.

The carriage stayed. I could not come.

This was the first of four evenings I spent at my uncle's house rather than on the east face of Ashworth, and I want to be honest about what those evenings were like without being self-indulgent about it, because there is a version of this accounting that makes me sound more romantic and less foolish than I actually was. The honest version is as follows: I worked on the documentation, which I did adequately. I took long walks in the direction of Ashworth and then, upon reaching a point from which the manor's chimneys were visible, turned around, which I did twice. I wrote three letters — one to my mother, one to my brother Robert, one to Eleanor — and sent one of them, which was the one to my mother and which was entirely about the weather and the progress of the boundary dispute and contained nothing of any use to anyone.

The letters to Robert and Eleanor I did not send. Robert's I burned. Eleanor's I still have, which is not something I examined the implications of for several years.

I saw Sir Phillip Morrow at church on the Sunday. He was in the Vane pew, to Gerald Vane's left, with Eleanor to his right. I was in the third row of the nave, to the left of the aisle, which gave me an unobstructed view of the right side of his face for the duration of the service.

He was perhaps fifty. Well-made, in the manner of a man who had always been prosperous enough to be well-made — good coat, good posture, the ease in the shoulders of someone who has never had to consider whether he belonged in a room. His hair was grey and neat. His hands, folded on the pew in front of him, were large and still.

He did not look at Eleanor during the service. I spent considerable time determining whether this was propriety or indifference and could not, from the nave, resolve the question. He looked at the vicar when the vicar was speaking. He looked at his prayer book when the prayer book was indicated. He did not look at Eleanor. Eleanor looked at the middle distance with the expression she wore when her mind was doing something other than what the situation required of it, and during the third hymn she had found, from somewhere, a stub of pencil, and I watched her draw something small on the inside of her glove.

After the service, in the churchyard: Vane and Morrow standing together near the south porch, speaking with the ease of men conducting satisfactory business. I observed this from a position near the east wall, between a chest tomb and a yew that provided adequate cover for a man who had no reason to be loitering. Morrow spoke and Vane listened. Vane spoke and Morrow nodded, once, in the manner of a man who finds the information consistent with his existing expectations.

Eleanor stood slightly to the side of them, perhaps four feet, where the path curved toward the gate. She was not looking at Morrow or at her father. She was looking at a headstone — a modest one, eighteenth century, considerably weathered — and she had taken off her right glove, the one she had drawn on, and she was tracing the edge of the lichen growth across the inscription with one bare finger.

I watched her trace it.

I went back to my uncle's house and sat in the chair by the window and looked at the boundary documentation and thought about very little of use.

On the Tuesday evening, four days after the carriage on the Ashworth drive, I was reading — or holding a book in the configuration of reading — when there was a knock at my uncle's door that was different from the housekeeper's knock in its rhythm and urgency. I opened it to find Harriet Vane on the doorstep, alone, in her outdoor coat, slightly out of breath.

She had not done this before. The singularity of it occupied approximately two seconds before I stepped back and let her in.

She declined to sit. She stood in the hallway and delivered her message with the efficiency of someone who had a specific task and intended to complete it: Sir Phillip's carriage had departed that morning. Eleanor wanted Edmund to come tomorrow night. Those were the words: *Eleanor wants Edmund to come tomorrow night.* She then appeared to consider whether there was anything further required of her, decided there was not, and moved back toward the door.

I asked how Eleanor had seemed. During the visit. In general.

Harriet stopped. She had the look, briefly, of someone performing an honest calculation rather than a reassuring one, which did not comfort me.

She said: "Like herself, mostly. A little more so."

I turned this over. I said I wasn't sure what that meant.

She said she wasn't sure it was the kind of thing that meant something in the way I was looking for. Then she left. I stood in my uncle's hallway for a moment, her absence very present, the cold from the open door still in the air.

The following evening I came to the east face to find the Virginia creeper further diminished — the section I had been relying on above the lintel was now entirely gone, stripped back to bare stem, and I had to find a new approach, further left, that added four minutes and a moment near the second-floor ledge where I was committed in a way I found clarifying in the manner of experiences that are not quite fear but have fear's texture. By the time I reached the ledge I was breathing harder than the climb explained.

The window was unlatched. Of course it was. I rested my forehead briefly against the cold glass, and then I lifted and pushed, and went in.
step_outputs.10:
# Chapter Five — *What She Said*

She was quieter than I had seen her.

Not unhappy, or not only that — Eleanor's unhappiness, when it came, came with precision, with specific complaint, with the energy of a woman who found vagueness in her own feelings professionally unsatisfying. This was different. She was sitting in the chair nearest the fire, which she did not usually do — she usually sat at the desk, or on the window seat with the ammonites at her elbow — and she had no book and no paper and her hands were in her lap doing nothing, which I had not seen before.

I came in. I closed the window. She looked up and said hello and looked back at the fire.

I sat down. I had prepared, on the walk to Ashworth and on the climb, a series of possible openings — the boundary dispute, which had developed a new absurdity I had been saving; a question about the Derwent survey; something I had read in the newspaper that I thought might produce an opinion. I looked at her profile in the firelight and all of it seemed, suddenly, like furniture moved to fill a room that needed emptying.

I said: "Tell me."

She said Morrow had known she was interested in geology. Her father had told him — mentioned it, she said, at some point during the correspondence between the two households, geology being the kind of fact about a daughter that transfers easily, that categorises without requiring understanding. She said Morrow had raised it once at dinner, in the first evening of his visit. He had said: *I understand you have an interest in natural history*.

She paused.

I waited.

She said he had not asked anything further. He had noted it, in the manner of noting something on a list — here is the entry, confirmed — and then moved on to something else, and she had sat at her father's dinner table and answered questions she hadn't been asked and eaten food she could not now recall and thought about the Devonian exposure on the lower bank with a focus that had surprised even her.

I said: "His eyes."

She looked at me. I said she had told me, in August, that his eyes didn't move when someone was speaking to him. She said yes. She said she had observed, over four days, that this was consistent.

Then she said, quietly, that she was not telling me this so that I would feel better about Morrow. She said she was aware that a man who didn't listen at dinner was still, in the relevant practical and social senses, entirely qualified to be a husband in this county in this decade, and that her father's assessment of the situation was not wrong in its terms, only in — she stopped. She said: "Only in whether the terms are the right ones."

I said: "They're not."

She looked at me and I knew I had been too quick, that she hadn't wanted agreement so much as witness, and I said nothing further.

The fire. The ammonites on the ledge, oldest to youngest, the candlelight not quite reaching the wall where the oldest ones were. The particular creak of the house at this hour, the one from the north staircase that I had catalogued and now recognised as structural, not human.

Then I said: "I want to tell you something. It isn't — I'm not telling you to argue for anything, or not primarily. I want you to have the information."

She turned toward me slightly.

I told her about the property in Shropshire. Not for the first time — I had described it before, briefly, in a general way. This time I told her the numbers. The acreage: twelve, of which seven were productive. The house: habitable, the east wing requiring a new roof which I was putting off while the practice built toward affording it. The practice: three years old, paying its own costs as of the previous quarter, not yet paying much beyond that, but genuinely. A junior partnership that might, in two or three years, become something more. The income, total, named as a figure. I said it plainly, the way I would say a figure in a legal document — without apology and without elaboration, just the number, which was what it was.

She listened. She did not perform listening; she simply did it.

When I finished, she was quiet for a moment. Then she asked what the property was called.

I said: Colton Hill.

She said: "And the land. What's underneath it."

I said I didn't know. I knew it was farming land, I knew the soil in that part of Shropshire, but I had not thought to look at the rock beneath it with any system. I had not, until three weeks ago, known enough to think about it that way.

She seemed to consider this. I watched her consider it. She had a way of going somewhere briefly, in her own mind, and then coming back — not checked out in the manner of inattention, but genuinely away and then genuinely returned. When she came back she looked at me directly.

She said: "Could I? Know it properly."

It took me a moment to understand that she meant the land. The geology. The rock beneath Colton Hill.

I said: "Yes."

She said: "I'd need access to the lower fields. And I'd want to follow the water table, which might require — "

I said: "Eleanor."

She stopped.

I said: "Yes. All of it. However it needs to be done."

She looked at me for a moment. There was something in her expression that I find, even now, difficult to name without misrepresenting it. Not relief, exactly — she was not a woman who had been waiting to be rescued and here was rescue. Something more like the look of someone who has done a long calculation and found that the answer is what they hoped but had not let themselves expect.

She said: "You haven't asked me anything."

I said: "No."

"Are you going to?"

I said: "I don't think it would be fair to ask you for anything formal until I've spoken to your father. Which I can't do until I've settled the boundary dispute, which — " I paused. "Which I have been delaying."

She said: "I know you've been delaying it."

I said: "You know."

"You've been here three months, Edmund. It's a fence."

There was a moment, and then she laughed — the silent shoulders first, the brief convulsion, and then the actual sound of it, quiet but real — and I laughed too, at myself, at the fence, at the three months, at the whole extraordinary circumstance of sitting in a house I had entered through the window discussing a boundary dispute with a woman I intended to marry.

When it settled, she looked at the fire. I looked at her. Nothing formal had been said; nothing formal was needed. We both knew where we were.

The climb down, that night, was easy. The cold didn't register. At the base of the east wall I looked up at the window and thought: that is the easiest thing I have done in three months.

The window went dark. I walked back through the grounds with the feeling, very clear and very simple, of a man who knows what he is doing and where he is going, which was not a feeling I had much previous experience of and which I found, I must say, extremely pleasant.
step_outputs.11:
# Chapter Six — *Mrs. Beale*

November committed itself. The creeper was gone entirely from the east face by the fifth of the month — I noted the date because I noted everything by this point, the professional habit of documentation having colonised what had begun as a personal situation — and what remained was bare stone and the drainpipe and the ledge and the necessity of technique over assistance. I had developed, by this stage, an approach to the drainpipe that I will describe only as involving my knees in a manner that was effective and wholly without elegance, and which I had determined never to describe to another living person. I am describing it now only because honesty requires acknowledging that the romantic enterprise of secret courtship has a physical dimension that the romantic literature of the period conspicuously fails to address.

It was colder. My uncle had begun to make remarks about the lateness of my returns, not accusatory, just observational, in the mild way he had — *you were out late again, Edmund* — and I had been saying yes, quite, and finding this insufficient and adding something about the night air, and my uncle had been accepting this with the kindness of a man who had decided, some time ago, to extend me a very long rope and trust my judgment about what to do with it.

The visits had continued. After the evening of the fire and the Shropshire acreage and the thing that had been understood without being formally stated, there had been two further evenings: one in which we had talked about the legal status of mineral rights on private land, which Eleanor had raised with the focused interest of someone asking for professional rather than theoretical reasons, and one in which she had produced, from her desk, a hand-drawn cross-section of the Derwent valley geology that covered three sheets of paper and which she spread across the floor and talked through for an hour while I sat on the rug and followed her as well as I could and asked questions that she answered in the patient tone of someone who had expected the gaps.

Both evenings: the ribbon system, Harriet's brief surveillance, the window, the cold, the drainpipe, the return. The extraordinary becoming ordinary, as it does, and the ordinary being, in this case, something I found I wanted to continue indefinitely.

The evening of the three ribbons was a Thursday.

I came to the east face and looked at the windows. Left window, Harriet's designated signal window. I looked at the ribbons — white, three of them, which I could see from below in the thin moonlight — and I could not determine what three meant. The system accommodated one ribbon and two ribbons in specific configurations. We had not discussed three. Three was not in the documentation, such as it was. I stood in the dark considering the epistemological problem of a signal system that had produced a signal outside its own parameters.

I thought: no ribbon means clear by other intelligence, at Harriet's discretion. I thought: three ribbons is not no ribbon and is not any ribbon we have specified. I thought: Harriet is sixteen and precise and does not make accidental signals.

I should have retreated. I know this. I knew it then, in a technical sense, the way one knows a legal argument has a weakness — intellectually, without it yet affecting one's behaviour.

I climbed.

I was in through the window and into the small anteroom adjacent to Eleanor's sitting room when I heard Mrs. Beale in the corridor.

Her footsteps were distinctive — I knew them by now, had catalogued them in the way I had catalogued the north staircase creak: even, measured, unhurried, the footsteps of someone who was not looking for anything in particular and was therefore liable to look at everything. I heard them in the corridor beyond the anteroom door and I stopped. I did not move. I did not breathe, or breathed in the way that amounts to not breathing — slow, controlled, aware of each component of it.

The anteroom was small and dark. I knew from prior evenings that it contained: a chair, a side table with nothing currently on it, and a defunct coal scuttle that I had identified by means of walking into it on my second visit.
